title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Chemotherapy
content: Sorafenib is the first-line treatment for the patient with BCLC advanced stage (C) with preserved liver function, ECOG-PS score of 1-2, and macrovascular invasion or extrahepatic spread. Sorafenib is a multikinase inhibitor. Sorafenib hepatocellular carcinoma assessment randomized protocol (SHARP) trial demonstrated a median survival of 10.7 months for patients receiving sorafenib as compared to 7.9 months in the placebo group. [57] In addition, sorafenib was shown to be effective in the Asia-Pacific region with patients having advanced HCC. [58] Common side effects of sorafenib include palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, diarrhea, weight loss, and hypertension. Lenvatinib was demonstrated noninferiority, but not superior to sorafenib. [59] Lenvatinib has been approved as the second agent for the first-line treatment of advanced HCC by the food and drug authority (FDA). Lenvatinib is associated with significant weight loss but less palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia when compared to sorafenib. Patients who are intolerant or have tumor progression on sorafenib are started on the second line of treatment. Regorafenib, which is a multikinase inhibitor, has been approved by the FDA as the second-line of treatment for advanced HCC. [60] Other second-line medications include cabozantinib, ramucirumab, and nivolumab, which is a programmed cell death 1 (PD-1) immune checkpoint inhibitor. [61] [62]

title: Lenvatinib -- Indications
content: The randomized, non-inferiority, phase III trial SELECT demonstrated lenvatinib use in HCC as non-inferior to sorafenib, a kinase inhibitor approved to treat HCC. The trial also demonstrated lenvatinib prolonged progression-free survival rate by 7.3 months and an objective response rate of 24% compared to sorafenib, 3.6 months and 9%, respectively. The SELECT phase III trial's efficacious results led to FDA approval for use as first-line therapy in unresectable or advanced HCC in patients not undergoing prior treatment. As per the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases, patients diagnosed with HCC and Child-Turcotte-Pugh A cirrhosis, for whom atezolizumab plus bevacizumab and durvalumab plus tremelimumab are inappropriate, should be offered sorafenib or lenvatinib as the primary treatment choices. A systematic review and meta-analysis examined the safety and efficacy of lenvatinib versus sorafenib for treating HCC within real-world scenarios. The study encompassed 9 single-arm investigations and 6 comparative studies. The results indicated that lenvatinib yielded notably extended overall and progression-free survival, with a higher objective response rate and disease control rate than sorafenib. However, lenvatinib exhibited a higher prevalence of hypertension than sorafenib, with no significant distinction in hand-foot syndrome. These findings suggest that lenvatinib may confer superior efficacy outcomes in treatment for HCC, although the safety profiles between the 2 drugs are comparable. [3] [4]

title: Liver Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
content: The Sorafenib Hepatocellular Carcinoma Assessment Randomized Protocol (SHARP) study, a multicenter, phase 3, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial with 602 patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma compared sorafenib (at a dose of 400 mg twice per day) to placebo and results revealed time to progression of 5.5 months versus 2.8 months and mOS of 10.7 months versus 7.9 months, in favor of sorafenib and statistically significant for both outcomes. Of note, the SHARP study only included hepatocellular carcinoma patients with Child-Pugh A, and a subgroup analysis showed HCV positive patients had a longer median survival of 14 months. Sorafenib was for long the only approved hepatocellular carcinoma option in the advanced disease setting until the Regorafenib After Sorafenib in Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma (RESORCE) study with regorafenib (a multikinase inhibitor) revealed positive results. This was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter phase 3, that enrolled 573 patients with Hepatocellular carcinoma whose disease has progressed after treatment with sorafenib greater than 400 mg/day and CP-A (97%). The trial compared 160 mg regorafenib once daily for 3 weeks on and 1 week off, or best supportive care plus placebo, with 28 days cycles. The results revealed a statistically significant median of survival of 10.6 months in the regorafenib group when compared with 7.8 months in the control arm. The Regorafenib After Sorafenib in Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma (RESORCE) study included 87% of patients with hepatocellular carcinoma and a Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer system stage C. Another option for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma whose disease progressed on sorafenib therapy is ramucirumab (a recombinant monoclonal antibody which inhibits vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2). In the REACH study, a median of survival was improved with ramucirumab (9.2 months) compared with placebo (7.6 months) but did not reach statistical significant (p = 0.1391). Nonetheless, a sub-analysis demonstrated a significant median of survival benefit for patients with an AFP level of 400 ng/mL or greater and Child-Pugh A for 7.8 months for ramucirumab compared with 4.2 months for placebo (HR, 0.67; 95% CI; 0.51, 0.90). REACH-2 study of ramucirumab in patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma with underlying Child-Pugh A cirrhosis and baseline AFP levels of 400 ng/mL or more is currently recruiting to evaluate this hypothesis. Ongoing clinical trials and future therapies are focused on immunotherapy (nivolumab and durvalumab) and small molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitors (cabozantinib). [15] [16] [17] [18]

title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Cabozantinib
content: It is a multitargeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor that inhibits VEGF, AXL, and MET kinase receptors. AXL and MET receptors have been postulated to be responsible for resistance to VEGF-targeted therapy, granted FDA and EMA approval for patients with progression on first-line VEGF targeted therapy. The inactivation of the Von Hippel Lindau gene leads to the activation of various angiogenic pathways, which may be blocked specifically by cabozantinib. The substrate of cytochrome P 450 enzyme system 3A4, predominantly metabolized in the liver. It is administered in a daily oral dose of 140 mg, with a predicted half-life of 55 hours. ESMO magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 3. [98] [104] [105]

title: Lenvatinib -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Lenvatinib is FDA-approved for the treatment of radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC), unresectable or advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), advanced renal cell carcinoma (RCC), and advanced endometrial carcinoma. The multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor demonstrates potent antiangiogenic properties. The inhibition of the VEGF receptors prevents tumor angiogenesis, and the inhibition of FGFR, RET, PDGFRa, and KIT prevents further proliferation of malignant cells. The prescribing team should include primary care clinicians, oncologists, nurses, and pharmacists, crucial for early diagnosis, monitoring potential adverse effects, and optimizing dosage adjustments based on renal function, liver enzymes, body weight, and cardiac health. Routine monitoring for adverse effects like hypertension, cardiac impairment, QT interval prolongation, thyroid function changes, and reproductive considerations is necessary, along with patient education for better treatment outcomes and increased quality of life.

content: Older patients: In patients with DTC, no differences in safety or efficacy have been marked compared to younger subjects. title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Sunitinib
content: It inhibits multiple receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR 1, 2, 3, PDGRF alpha, beta, c-kit, FLT-3, and RET. It is an orally administered drug with a half-life of 40-80 hours and a bioavailability of 50 percent. A single daily dose of 37.5 mg is administered for 4 weeks, followed by a drug holiday for 2 weeks. It is metabolized by the cytochrome 3a4 enzyme system in the liver, the active drug—excreted in the urine and feces. FDA and EMA approved in 2006. Sunitinib was approved in 2007 for use in low, intermediate, and high-risk groups as a first-line treatment option for advanced metastatic disease. Sunitinib has also shown benefit as a treatment option after progression on VEGF targeting agents. It has been studied in case of progression following sorafenib, and re-challenge following progression on multiple first-line settings. ESMO magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 4. [97] [98]

title: Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma -- Treatment / Management
content: Chemotherapy has served as a treatment tool before and following surgical resection. Due to the limited incidence of FL-HCC, no RCT has elucidated the most efficacious chemotherapeutic regimen. Of note is that no neoadjuvant/adjuvant systemic therapies have yet been reported, showing an improved survival benefit in patients with resectable FL-HCC. [15] Chemotherapy with agents such as gemcitabine, cisplatin, 5-fluorouracil, interferon, and oxaliplatin have to be utilized and have shown varying degrees of response. [18] [19] Combined treatment strategies, including surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation, have shown better outcomes. [20] Decreasing the mass size before surgical resection with percutaneous radioembolization has been studied. [21] A novel targeted therapy that has demonstrated efficacy in treating HCC, sorafenib, was evaluated retrospectively in FL-HCC, showing limited effectiveness. [19] Potential targets in the mTOR pathway and/or Aurora A kinase were recently identified. Currently, no controlled trials have evaluated these targets. There has been a favorable outcome in utilizing rapamycin (mTOR inhibitor) in an unresectable patient. [22] Checkpoint inhibition has shown reasonable efficacy in a Phase II study of advanced HCC. [23] Controlled trials evaluating checkpoint inhibitors in FL-HCC are lacking, and case reports to date are limited and unequivocal. [15] [19]

title: Lenvatinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
content: Hepatic impairment: As noted earlier, lenvaitnib is metabolized enzymatically in the liver by CYP450 isoform CYP3A and aldehyde oxidase. For patients with severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C), dose adjustment is required. For DTC, the prescribed dosage is 14 mg orally daily. For RCC, the recommended dosage is 10 mg orally daily. Similarly, for endometrial carcinoma, the suggested dosage is 10 mg orally daily.

title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Lenvatinib
content: Multitargeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor inhibits VEGFR 2, fibroblast growth factor receptor 1, 2, 3, 4, PDGFR alpha, c kit, and RET. FGFR inhibition, unique to lenvatinib (compared to sunitinib and sorafenib), provides an additional mechanism of inhibition of angiogenesis. It is administered orally at a dose of 24 mg daily, with an oral bioavailability of 90 percent. It has hepatic and renal metabolism and is excreted in the bile. Demethylated (M2) metabolite is mainly excreted via the urinary and fecal routes. ESMO magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 4. [98] [100]

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
content: The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1074
content: Citalopram is an antidepressant medication; it is not treated by alcohol dependence, and the suggestion that alcohol dependence could serve as therapy for citalopram is inaccurate. Alcohol dependence, however, is clinically associated with inflammation of the liver (hepatitis), and this relationship can exacerbate liver pathology. Hepatitis is classified as inflammation of the liver (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) and represents a key clinical condition that links several liver‑related diseases. Hepatitis is also clinically associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية), a specific manifestation of cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile ducts. In turn, hepatocellular carcinoma has a broader relationship with cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ), reflecting its place within the spectrum of serious liver disorders. Understanding these interconnections—alcohol dependence contributing to hepatitis, hepatitis predisposing to hepatocellular carcinoma, and hepatocellular carcinoma being part of broader cholestatic liver disease—helps clinicians assess risk factors, monitor disease progression, and devise comprehensive management strategies for patients with liver disease.
